A_intro:
certification include the following:
1. General education - approximately 45 semester hours. This must include
A_methods:
courses in English composition, health and physical education, science, humanities,
and social sciences.
2. Professional education - 24 to 26 semester hours. The amount will vary
some, depending upon the level of preparation.
3. Subject matter preparation - 45 semester hours. For high schcol preparation, the student will select a teaching major of approximately 30 semester hours
and a minor of approximately 15 semester hours. Courses suitable for majors and
minors are determined by the teacher training institution.
hk. Electives - 14 semester hours. A Bachelor's degree requiring at least
128 semester hours must be earned by all prospective teachers prior to certification.
A_results:
In addition to the basic four years of training, all Washington State teachers
will be expected to ccmplete a fifth year of education within five years.
The pattern of teacher training in agriculture will necessarily follow the
guidelines rather closely. Prospective teachers of vocational agriculture will
earn a minimum of 42 semester hours of credit in technical agriculture, exclusive
-22-
of courses in agricultural mechanics. Most prospective teachers will earn a teaching minor of 15 semester hours in farm mechanics. Some will also earn a minor in
biological science or in physical science.
Most teachers of vocational agriculture will complete the fifth year prior to
teaching experience. Hcwever, through careful planning some will qualify in four
years and @ summer session. A provisional certificate will be issued to beginning
agriculture teachers who have completed 148 semester hours of work.
All secondary school teachers will be expected to complete a minimum of 158
semester hours and two years of successful teaching in order to be eligible for
a standard certificate.
This new plan in teacher certification in the State of Washington is a result
of the demand for greater emphasis on subject matter preparation for teachers,
especially at the junior high school and senior high school levels of teaching.
